q: Which is considered more advanced, the butterfly or the caterpillar?
expected_text: But no one probably will dispute that the butterfly is higher than the caterpillar.

q: Have you found any terrestrial animals that fertilize themselves?

q: What did the author do to the aphides on the dock-plant?
expected_text: I removed all the ants from a group of about a dozen aphides on a dock-plant, and prevented their attendance during several hours.

q: What does each geological formation signify according to this view?
expected_text: Each formation, on this view, does not mark a new and complete act of creation, but only an occasional scene, taken almost at hazard, in a slowly changing drama.

q: Why is the reproductive process in hermaphrodites less obvious than in animals and plants with separated sexes?
expected_text: In the case of animals and plants with separated sexes, it is of course obvious that two individuals must always unite for each birth; but in the case of hermaphrodites this is far from obvious.

q: Have things remained unchanged since eternity?
expected_text: But have we any right to assume that things have thus remained from eternity?

q: What is the tendency of larger biological groups over time?
expected_text: Dominant species belonging to the larger groups tend to give birth to new and dominant forms; so that each large group tends to become still larger, and at the same time more divergent in character.

q: What effect does the laborious breathing necessary in high regions have on the size of the chest?

q: Has any variable ever ceased to be variable under cultivation?
expected_text: No case is on record of a variable being ceasing to be variable under cultivation.

expected_text: I see no reason to limit the process of modification, as now explained, to the formation of genera alone.

q: Are the breaks between species real or artificial?
expected_text: But these breaks are imaginary, and might have been inserted anywhere, after intervals long enough to have allowed the accumulation of a considerable amount of divergent variation.

q: How do rudimentary structures compare to other parts of the organization?

q: Has the dog's affection for humans evolved into an innate behavior?

q: What separates the sub-arctic and northern temperate productions of the Old and New Worlds?
expected_text: At the present day, the sub-arctic and northern temperate productions of the Old and New Worlds are separated from each other by the Atlantic Ocean and by the extreme northern part of the Pacific.

q: Why would a group of animals with less diverse organization struggle against one with greater structural diversity?
expected_text: A set of animals, with their organisation but little diversified, could hardly compete with a set more perfectly diversified in structure.

q: How do lower and higher members of a class compare in their rate of change?

q: Is there a single, universally accepted definition of a species?

q: How important are instincts compared to physical structure for a species' survival?
expected_text: It will be universally admitted that instincts are as important as corporeal structure for the welfare of each species, under its present conditions of life.

q: Which forms will naturally suffer most?

expected_text: This case seems to me well to illustrate the spirit with which our classifications are sometimes necessarily founded.
